raw_case:
CD-34 negative solitary fibrous tumor of the prostate: A case report
Solitary fibrous tumor is an uncommon myofibroblastic tumor that affects pleura. While most of these tumors are in the intrathoracic cavity, a small minority have been noted to be found elsewhere, including the prostate. In this case study, we present a patient who presented with obstructive urinary symptoms and was diagnosed with solitary fibrous tumor of the prostate on immunohistochemical staining.1

Introduction

Solitary fibrous tumor (SFT) is an uncommon myofibroblastic tumor that primarily affects the pleura. However, over the last decade, roughly 30 % of SFTs have been found in nonserosal and extrathoracic regions.1 Out of these tumors, a very small minority have been reported in the prostate. Around 40 cases of prostate SFT have been reported globally, and these tumors can be difficult to diagnose both clinically and pathologically as many of the symptoms, immunohistochemical, and histomorphological findings overlap with other neoplasms of the prostate. Recently, SFT has been associated with a mutation on chromosome 12q that results in a NAB2-STAT6 gene transfusion.2 This discovery could help clear up some of the confusion around diagnosing SFTs.Here, we report a case of a 76-year-old man initially diagnosed with and treated for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) who was later found to have prostate SFT.2

Case

Our patient is a 76-year-old male with a past medical history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and coronary atherosclerosis who presented to the urology clinic due to worsening urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia, and weak stream. He was initially diagnosed with BPH several years before and had been on tamsulosin for his symptoms but experienced no symptomatic relief. His prostate-specific antigen (PSA) at the time had ranged from 1.2 to 2.3 during this time with his last PSA being 1.2. A digital rectal exam showed a diffusely enlarged prostate. A transrectal ultrasound showed a prostate volume of 57.3 cc with no signs of tumor formation. The patient elected to undergo Holmium laser enucleation of the prostate (HoLEP), and 49g of prostate tissue was removed intraoperatively.Immunohistochemical staining of tissue collected during the HoLEP procedure stained positive for vimentin (Fig. 1: Panel A) and STAT6 (Fig. 1: Panel B), and a diagnosis of prostate solitary fibrous was given. Staining was negative for cytokeratin, PSA, NKX3.1, CD34 (Fig. 1: Panel C), CD10, PR, CD45, p63, Mart-1, sox 10, WT-1, synaptophysin, S100 and smooth muscle myosin heavy chain. Ki-67 showed positivity at a rate of 10–15 % (Fig. 1: panel D). Due to the HoLEP procedure, the tumor size could not be identified. A morphologic examination of the tissue revealed a stromal neoplasm with a mitotic rate >4 per 10 high power fields (Fig. 2). The tumor was identified as being of at least intermediate risk. Benign stromal hyperplasia, consistent with BPH was also found in the HoLEP specimen (Fig. 3). Due to the typical appearance of the benign stromal hyperplasia slides, no immunohistochemical staining was performed on them. There was no evidence of anything other malignancy, including prostatic adenocarcinoma, found in the HoLEP specimen.The staging CT was negative for any signs of metastasis. After a review of the literature and discussion with the patient, the decision was made to proceed with robot-assisted radical prostatectomy (RARP) with bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection due to there being limited data around the effectiveness of chemotherapy or radiotherapy on SFT. Final pathology from the RARP showed prostatic adenocarcinoma Gleason 3 + 4 pT2N0R0 without any residual solitary fibrous tumor being identified. Currently, the patient is asymptomatic and on regular follow-up with undetectable PSA levels.3

Discussion

Although mesenchymal prostate tumors account for less than 1 % of tumors, the accurate diagnosis and treatment of them is important to clinicians and pathologists as treatment and prognosis can vary widely between the different subtypes of tumors.3,4 Amongst the mesenchymal tumors of the prostate, solitary fibrous tumors are some of the rarest, with around 40 cases published globally.SFT can be a difficult diagnosis to make clinically as the presenting symptoms, physical exam findings, laboratory findings, and imaging results share common features with several more prevalent pathologies. Prostate SFT normally presents in middle-aged and elderly individuals with obstructive urinary symptoms such as increased frequency, urinary retention, and a weak stream. Moreover, a digital rectal exam will show a grossly enlarged prostate rather than a discrete tumor. PSA is often normal.5 These nonspecific findings can make diagnosis difficult and lead clinicians to the incorrect initial diagnosis. The best current treatment options include radical surgery with negative surgery margins because radiotherapy and chemotherapy have proven to be futile in these tumors.5Lastly, the absence of CD-34 is a pertinent finding in this case. While most SFTs stain positive for CD-34, CD-34-negative disease is often associated with high-grade disease and is more likely to present as metastatic disease.6 CD-34-negative SFT is more likely to present in the head and neck area, and, to the best of our knowledge, ours is the first case of CD-34-negative disease in the prostate. Given that this subtype of disease is associated with higher-grade disease and more aggressive behavior, surgical management should be performed in a timely manner.4

Conclusion

In this paper, we add to the literature another case of prostate SFT and describe the first case of CD34-negative prostatic solitary fibrous tumor. We present a case of prostate SFT in a 67-year-old male who was initially diagnosed with and treated for BPH. The diagnosis of SFT can be challenging as the presenting symptoms, physical exam findings, lab results, and cell morphology can be attributed to several other diseases and tumors. We highlight the use of STAT6 on immunohistochemical staining as an indicator of SFT and note that our case is the first to have CD34-negative disease.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Parker Heger: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Austin Hill: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Cameron Charchenko: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 76-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Worsening urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia, and weak urinary stream.
- **History of Present Illness:** Presented with progressive lower urinary tract symptoms despite treatment for presumed benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) since diagnosis several years prior. Symptoms were unresponsive to tamsulosin.
- **Past Medical History:** Type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, coronary atherosclerosis.
- **Personal History:** None noted.
- **Family History:** None noted.
- **Physical Examination:** Digital rectal exam showed a diffusely enlarged prostate.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - PSA ranged from 1.2 to 2.3 during prior evaluations; most recent PSA was 1.2.
  - Transrectal ultrasound showed prostate volume of 57.3 cc without tumor formation.
  - Post-Holmium laser enucleation of the prostate (HoLEP), immunohistochemical staining revealed:
    - Positive for vimentin and STAT6.
    - Negative for cytokeratin, PSA, NKX3.1, CD34, CD10, PR, CD45, p63, Mart-1, sox 10, WT-1, synaptophysin, S100, and smooth muscle myosin heavy chain.
    - Ki-67 positivity at 10–15%.
    - Morphological findings: stromal neoplasm with mitotic rate >4 per 10 high-power fields.
    - Benign stromal hyperplasia consistent with BPH also noted.
  - Staging CT was negative for metastasis.
  - Final pathology post-RARP revealed prostatic adenocarcinoma Gleason 3+4 pT2N0R0 without residual solitary fibrous tumor.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Presentation with Lower Urinary Obstructive Symptoms:**
   - BPH was the leading suspected diagnosis due to the patient's age, presentation, and diffusely enlarged prostate. However, lack of symptom relief from tamsulosin and normal PSA levels prompted further investigation.
   - Prostate adenocarcinoma was considered but was excluded by initial immunohistochemical staining, which was negative for PSA and associated markers.

2. **Histopathological Analysis Post-HoLEP:**
   - The positive staining for STAT6 supported solitary fibrous tumor (SFT) as a likely diagnosis. The benign stromal hyperplasia seen in additional specimens was consistent with BPH but did not explain the aggressive pathological findings of a stromal neoplasm with high mitotic activity.
   - The tumor's negativity for CD34, although rare, strengthened the diagnosis of a high-grade SFT. Other differentials like sarcomas or neuroendocrine tumors were ruled out due to negative markers (e.g., synaptophysin, S100).

3. **Exclusion of Metastasis and Confirmation Through Surgery:**
   - Staging CT ruled out metastatic disease.
   - Final pathology post-RARP confirmed no residual SFT and only detected low-grade adenocarcinoma (Gleason 3+4).

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Prostatic solitary fibrous tumor (CD34-negative, intermediate risk) and concurrent low-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 3+4 pT2N0R0).

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Prostatic solitary fibrous tumor (CD34-negative) and prostatic adenocarcinoma (Gleason 3+4 pT2N0R0).

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Initial Presentation and Suspicion of BPH:** The patient is a 76-year-old male presenting with worsening lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) such as urinary frequency, urgency, nocturia, and weak stream. These symptoms are highly suggestive of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), especially in this age group, and the patient was indeed previously treated for presumed BPH. The diffusely enlarged prostate on digital rectal exam (DRE) further supports this initial clinical impression.

<step 2> **Unresponsiveness to Tamsulosin and Further Investigations:** The patient's symptoms were unresponsive to tamsulosin, an alpha-blocker commonly used to treat BPH. This lack of response, coupled with worsening symptoms, necessitates further investigation to rule out other underlying pathologies or complications of BPH.  PSA levels were monitored and remained relatively low (1.2-2.3), which, while reassuring for typical aggressive prostate cancer, does not exclude other prostatic pathologies. Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) was performed, revealing an enlarged prostate volume consistent with BPH, and initially reported no tumor formation.

<step 3> **Unexpected Finding of Solitary Fibrous Tumor (SFT) after HoLEP:**  The patient underwent Holmium laser enucleation of the prostate (HoLEP), a surgical procedure to treat BPH.  Post-HoLEP pathological examination revealed a surprising finding: immunohistochemical staining and morphological features consistent with a stromal neoplasm, specifically a solitary fibrous tumor (SFT). The positive staining for vimentin and STAT6, coupled with the negativity for epithelial and other mesenchymal markers, strongly points towards SFT. The mitotic rate >4 per 10 high-power fields and Ki-67 positivity of 10-15% indicate a degree of cellular proliferation.  Importantly, benign stromal hyperplasia consistent with BPH was also noted, confirming the initial clinical suspicion of BPH as a concurrent condition.

<step 4> **Further Investigation and Radical Prostatectomy (RARP):** Despite the HoLEP and SFT finding, the patient's underlying urinary symptoms and possibly other clinical factors prompted further definitive management.  A staging CT was performed, which was negative for metastasis, suggesting localized disease at that point.  Subsequently, a Radical Prostatectomy (RARP) was performed.

<step 5> **Final Diagnosis of Prostatic Adenocarcinoma:** The final pathology report after RARP revealed the definitive diagnosis: prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 3+4, pT2N0R0. This indicates a moderately differentiated, organ-confined prostate cancer with no lymph node metastasis and negative surgical margins.  Crucially, the RARP pathology report explicitly states "without residual solitary fibrous tumor." This suggests that the SFT identified in the HoLEP specimen was likely completely removed during the HoLEP procedure itself and was an incidental finding in the context of the ultimately diagnosed prostate cancer. The prostatectomy was performed to address the adenocarcinoma.

<step 6> **Synthesizing Findings and Final Diagnostic Conclusion:**  The patient presented with LUTS initially attributed to BPH. While BPH was confirmed pathologically, an unexpected Solitary Fibrous Tumor was also found after HoLEP.  However, the definitive and clinically most significant diagnosis established by the final pathology after RARP is prostatic adenocarcinoma.  The SFT appears to have been a separate, incidental finding that was addressed during the HoLEP, while the adenocarcinoma was the primary driver for the subsequent radical prostatectomy and is the final diagnosis.

### Answer:
Prostatic Adenocarcinoma, Gleason 3+4, pT2N0R0
